paragraphs.body: Short Story 2 (Title TBD)
The sun was nearly at its peak by the time Patti reached the front of the line. Her son, Linell, sat at her feet, ripping the overgrown grass of the lawn into thin strips.

They, like the rest of the crowd, paid little mind to the commotion coming from inside of the tent. It was not that Patti and the others did not feel for the person screaming– they did, of course, have decent human empathy. But there was only so much a person could take on and, in times like these, everyone had their own worries.

annotations.comment: I love how this takes me into the world of this story right away. I can’t wait to find out what’s going on.

paragraphs.body: The tent they were camped in front of was much like the half dozen others dotting the flat park ground. Government issued, monstrously large, and a pukish orange color, they were made specifically equipped to handle natural disasters. All around the tents people swelled, standing or sitting in rudimentary lines that seemed to grow tenfold with each passing hour.

annotations.comment: Could we know what they are waiting to get or find out inside the tent? Or at least what they think they’re waiting for?

paragraphs.body: Doctors and volunteers darted between the crowds, pushing large carts and carrying clipboards, their fluorescent protective suits illuminated in the light of the sun. A group of volunteers had come by hours before, passing out blankets and water bottles.

Still, despite all of this, a pervasive sense of panic undermined the crowd. Patti could feel it in the nervous shifting of the people around her, the crying, the helplessness. Search and rescue teams were still combing through the wreckage.

annotations.comment: Again, still want to know what’s inside the tent. Otherwise, so good!

paragraphs.body: The line to the emergency tent was moving at a deadening pace, the health care volunteers accepting a patient every twenty or so minutes. Patti understood the process took time but in that moment, it all felt unbearably trivial.
She checked her watch and swore. It was 12:17– roughly ten hours since she and Linell had arrived. Ten hours and still they had yet to have the chance to speak to anyone that knew anything.

annotations.comment: Arrived and gotten in line? Maybe?
annotations.context_right: her watch and swore. It was 12:17– roughly ten hours since she and Linell had arrived. Ten hours an

paragraphs.body: Patti looked down at her son, stuck in a contradiction. His small head was bent low, eyes focused and thin shoulders hunched as carefully blew small whiffs into a blade of grass. No sound escaped, to his obvious annoyance, save for the gurgle of spit that splattered some feet away.

annotations.comment: Is the line moving? Has it slowed enough that it’s possible for this kid to just flop down and wait? Just trying to follow that part of it.

paragraphs.body: For a second, Patti almost laughed at the normalcy of the action. His face was its usual rosy color, void of any of the feverish pallor he'd exhibited the day before. Patti knew the signs of the sickness well. Fever came first, everyone knew that. Then the rashes, the headaches, the nausea. She’d been in a state of panic, yesterday, when Linell had started to display the symptoms. She’d  watched the news, seen how fast the sickness spread. Fear had overtaken her, and she’d rushed him here without thinking.

annotations.comment: Maybe “had started to run a fever” here?

paragraphs.body: But as soon as they arrived, his fever broke. After a good meal and some sleep, Linell was back to his usual self. Yet, Patti hesitated to leave. She had to be sure.

annotations.comment: I was surprised that he had a good meal in line?Also, a small matter, but I had thought that they were fleeing a natural disaster? No — more of a pandemic?

paragraphs.body: Going to the family physician was out of the question. According to the receptionist at their local doctor’s office, they and every other office in the county were at capacity, with a waitlist of at least nine months.

The hospital would be worse. The whole town of Mayfield had seen the news in the days following the flood, when the first threads of the sickness had begun to spread. The water was contaminated, and the hospitals were overwhelmed. The number of cases had only grown since then.

annotations.comment: Ah, okay, this helps me follow it.
Spread from contaminated water…Was the gov’s water contaminated or did they have to drink the flood water?

paragraphs.body: Patti itched to reach her hand down, into her jean pocket, where she knew a pack of cigarettes lay waiting. Frustration bubbled inside of her. She didn’t smoke, hadn’t for years, but with everything going on these days who could blame her?

annotations.comment: Why does she have cigarettes then?

paragraphs.body: The sobs emanating from the tent, loud and sharp in the chill October air, continued in a constant ringing in her ear. It gnawed at her. Her skin was on fire, itchy. She pulled up her sleeves, resisting the urge to scratch. She wanted to scream– at the impossible slowness of the line, at the workers for not moving fast enough, at Mayfield’s broken healthcare system, at herself for not being able to afford decent insurance that would allow her to not have to wait in this stupid line.

annotations.comment: Why are people crying? I’d like a better sense of what Patti wants — why is she in line? Is it to get Linnell looked at? (Which makes sense) Or some other reason?
Would she be better off with insurance? It sounds like the doctors and hospitals are full no matter what?

paragraphs.body: Her fingertip grazed the lacquered paper of the box in her pocket, curling to unclip the paper latch, when she felt a tug on the bottom of her jacket.
She glanced down. Linell, apparently having given up on the particular grass blade he had been coddling, looked up and met her gaze with drooping eyes. “I’m bored, Mom.”
Patti took a deep breath, her hand slipping out from her pocket and resting on his shoulder. “I know, honey. We can leave soon.”

annotations.comment: Where would they go? Do they have a safe home?

paragraphs.body: “But why not now? I feel fine.”
Patti smiled, though it didn’t reach her eyes. “I just want to be sure.”
“Can’t I go play with the other kids?” He looked longing towards the distant structure at the center of the park.

Patti cringed as she followed the root of his gaze. A curious number of children, as Linell had alluded, seemed to be running and whooping in loud shrieks between the lines of people waiting to be processed in that area.
“You know the answer, Linell. Please, let it rest.” She rubbed her temples, attempting to dispel the dull throbbing in her skull.
“But–”
“I said no.”

Linell slumped, but said nothing more, his gaze still fixed on the distant structure.  Patti’s gaze lingered on the scene as well– the irony of the image was surreal, apocalyptical even.  She watched a small child duck beneath one of the many cream-colored cots that had been strung along the sparse treeline. There was a strange dissonance in the scene– children playing while the sick and mourning waited, crowded together like cattle. It felt wrong, somehow.

annotations.comment: Very creepy image. But I did wonder about what the not-sick people were there for?

paragraphs.body: Patti turned her attention back to the tent as the entrance opened at last. Linell dropped his strands completely, his work from the previous hour forgotten at the sight of the lady that emerged. She was older, perhaps in her late sixties, her pale, weathered face flushed with tears. She blinked into the sunlight, as if in a daze, her blue eyes glassy. She seemed to be muttering something, a plea, under her breath.

Patti could make out a number of small, raised bumps on the woman’s bare arms– a progressive sign of infection. An attendant held her by her shoulders, hands carefully gloved, pushing the woman with a polite, but firm, nudge towards the parking lot and bus area. The woman dug her heels into the dirt.

annotations.comment: I wanted to hear what she might say.

paragraphs.body: “Fucking hell,” someone behind Patti muttered. “We don’t have all day.”
Patti frowned, stomach twisting, as the woman clutched the slim piece of paper in her spidery grip. It was red. Terminal, then.

annotations.comment: You might play with the idea that they wouldn’t usually walk this woman out of the tent in front of everyone (dangerous for contagion!) but they have to here for some reason?

paragraphs.body: The volunteer from before returned moments later with a new clipboard and a forced smile. “Who’s up next?”
Patti nudged Linell, pulling him to his feet. “My son.”

annotations.comment: I think I would make more of the fact that they’re at the front of the line by now. It’s not mentioned.

paragraphs.body: “Just him? Are you sure you don’t want to–”
“ I’m fine,” Patti cut in, her tone flat. Her head was killing her. She wanted to get this over with. “Just him.”

annotations.comment: Why wouldn’t they test her too? It’s a contagious disease, right?

paragraphs.body: The woman gave her a sideways glance but said nothing. She led them through a passage to a small room with thin canvas walls. Small fluorescent lights buzzed overhead, casting a sterile glow on Linell’s angular face.

The room was equipped with a basic, if slightly sparse, examination station, with a loosely lined bed, an IV stand, and an assortment of medical supplies that were stacked neatly on a metal cart. The walls were lined with posters about hygiene and immunizations, with a paper gown sat folded neatly on a side chair.

The doctor entered after a few minutes, his movements brisk and efficient. Patti tried her best to stay still as he worked. He took a number of tests of various uses: on Linell’s balance, his eyesight, his throat, the list went on and on. Linell complied with all his demands without complaint.

Patti barely registered the process. She was a mess of nerves by the end. Sweat beaded on her brow, her pulse quickening with each passing second. She could hear the hushed conversations of others in the rooms around them—mothers comforting children, patients murmuring to their companions about their worries, the soft click of instruments– in the back of her mind. She thought she may choke.
The whole examination took less than ten minutes, but it felt like hours.

When it was over, and the doctor awarded Patti a green sheet proclaiming Linell’s health, a significant weight lifted from Patti’s chest. She pulled Linell into a tight hug, overjoyed as the relief washed over her in a rush.

annotations.comment: I felt genuine relief here.

paragraphs.body: But as she moved to leave, a young woman, the attendant from before, stood before her in the entrance of the tent.
“Excuse, ma’am, can I hold you for a second.” The woman looked to be in her late twenties. She smiled brightly at Linell, gesturing for him to wait for them both outside.
“Is something wrong?”
“I just need a second of your time. Nothing more”
Patti squinted for a moment, considering, before nodding to her son. “Meet me right outside of the tent, okay? No straying.”
Linell nodded, yawning. “I won’t, Mom.”
Patti turned back to the woman. The volunteer’s easy expression was gone. She took a deep breath, as though preparing herself to speak. “Has the fever started yet?”
“What?” Patti’s breath quickened. “What does this have to do with Linell? I thought you said he was okay.”
“He is.” The woman looked pained. “This is about you, ma’am. Your arm… the rash. “I saw it when you were in line.”
“This isn’t happening,” Patti couldn’t think, couldn’t breathe. She wasn’t sick, she knew she wasn’t. She had had whatever Linell had, that’s all.

annotations.comment: Does Patti notice the rash for the first time here?

paragraphs.body: The doctor’s expression was strained. She moved to put a hand on her shoulder, and then thought better of it. Patti jerked away.
“Please,” the volunteer’s tone was gentle, soothing. A slap in the face. “Let us run some tests.”
“I can’t–” Patti whispered, her voice thick.

annotations.comment: I would assume that there would be some mechanism where they just run the test without her permission?

paragraphs.body: “You need to prepare for what is going to happen. You–” she paused at the look on her face. “I really am sorry.”
“No.” Patti’s voice was shaky. She turned without another word.
The sun was bright as she exited the tent. The light burned her eyes, and for a moment, she thought she might collapse. The rush of emotions was too much, everything was too much.
She found Linell sitting by the base of a tree some yards from the entrance. He stood when he saw her. Rushed to her. “They made me move, Mom, it wasn’t me. I was in the way. I-”
Linell stopped when he saw her face. “Mom, don’t cry. I’m okay. The doctor said everything is going to be okay.”
Patti could feel her eyes starting to tear. She wrapped her arms around him, let herself memorize the feeling of his warmth. She could feel her heart breaking.
“I know it is, honey. I know.”

annotations.comment: Maddy, this is a strong first draft. I enjoyed the way you captured how boring a horrible disease can be — we’ve forgotten that from the pandemic. You can only be terrified for so long before you just get… bored. I do think that the story lacks suspense after they go into the tent and that you can set things up a bit better at first to make it land more at the end. I’d clarify what they’re in line for — it was hard to know if it was for food, shelter, medical stuff, etc. at first. I might play with the idea that the rash as symptom isn’t known yet so that Patti doesn’t know she has a symptom (it could also be something that only shows up in adults, etc.). You might also think about whether she does not notice or is in high denial. And I think I would be more distressed at the end if I knew a little bit more about their situation — is she Linell’s only adult? The thing of it is, it feels so irresponsible of her to not acknowledge her illness, so we have to better understand why she doesn’t admit it. What would keep her from that, besides fear? If she’s just overwhelmed, maybe the story ends a little sooner, so we get the shock she feels and then it’s over? Final note: is this disease contagious? No one seems to really act like it is…Great work, Maddy. Excited to see your revision.
